findings.title: Redis < 8.2.1 lua script - Integer Overflow
findings.description: Redis is an open source, in-memory database that persists on disk. Versions 8.2.1 and below allow an authenticated user to use a specially crafted Lua script to cause an integer overflow and potentially lead to remote code execution The problem exists in all versions of Redis with Lua scripting. This issue is fixed in version 8.2.2.

findings.impact: Authenticated users can execute arbitrary code remotely, potentially compromising the entire system.

findings.title: Redis Lua Parser < 8.2.2 - Use After Free
findings.description: Redis is an open source, in-memory database that persists on disk. Versions 8.2.1 and below allow an authenticated user to use a specially crafted Lua script to manipulate the garbage collector, trigger a use-after-free and potentially lead to remote code execution. The problem exists in all versions of Redis with Lua scripting. This issue is fixed in version 8.2.2. To workaround this issue without patching the redis-server executable is to prevent users from executing Lua scripts. This can be done using ACL to restrict EVAL and EVALSHA commands.

findings.impact: Authenticated attackers can manipulate the garbage collector through crafted Lua scripts to trigger use-after-free conditions, potentially achieving remote code execution on Redis servers.

findings.title: Redis  < 8.2.1 Lua Long-String Delimiter - Out-of-Bounds Read
findings.description: Redis is an open source, in-memory database that persists on disk. Versions 8.2.1 and below allow an authenticated user to use a specially crafted LUA script to read out-of-bound data or crash the server and subsequent denial of service. The problem exists in all versions of Redis with Lua scripting. This issue is fixed in version 8.2.2. To workaround this issue without patching the redis-server executable is to prevent users from executing Lua scripts. This can be done using ACL to block a script by restricting both the EVAL and FUNCTION command families.

findings.title: Redis - Default Logins
findings.description: Redis service was accessed with easily guessed credentials.

findings.impact: Attackers may read/write database contents and potentially achieve remote code execution depending on configuration.
findings.remediation: Restrict Redis to trusted networks (firewall/VPC rules), bind to localhost or a private interface, and require authentication (ACLs). Disable dangerous commands if not needed.

findings.title: Redis Lua Sandbox < 8.2.2 - Cross-User Escape
findings.description: Redis is an open source, in-memory database that persists on disk. Versions 8.2.1 and below allow an authenticated user to use a specially crafted Lua script to manipulate different LUA objects and potentially run their own code in the context of another user. The problem exists in all versions of Redis with LUA scripting. This issue is fixed in version 8.2.2. A workaround to mitigate the problem without patching the redis-server executable is to prevent users from executing LUA scripts. This can be done using ACL to block a script by restricting both the EVAL and FUNCTION command families.

findings.impact: Attackers may read/write database contents and potentially achieve remote code execution depending on configuration.
findings.remediation: Restrict Redis to trusted networks (firewall/VPC rules), bind to localhost or a private interface, and require authentication (ACLs). Disable dangerous commands if not needed.

findings.impact: Missing browser-side mitigations can increase the impact of XSS, clickjacking, and related attacks.
findings.remediation: Add recommended security headers (e.g., CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options/Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options) at the web server or application layer.

findings.title: WordPress End-of-Life - Detect
findings.description: Detected WordPress versions that have reached End-of-Life (EOL) and no longer receive security updates.

evidence.name: WordPress End-of-Life - Detect

findings.impact: End-of-life software may contain unpatched vulnerabilities, increasing the likelihood and impact of exploitation.
findings.remediation: Upgrade the affected platform to a supported (non-EOL) version and apply the latest security patches.
